channel.title: 8. Comparisons on Arnesh's Website
channel.description: Recent content in 8. Comparisons on Arnesh's Website

item.title: 8.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content It’s hard to get through the Verbal section of the GMAT without encountering Sentence Correction questions that involve comparisons. In a comparison, two or more things, ideas, concepts, entities, or people are compared. For example: John is like Melissa; they both love Caribbean beaches. The teeth of the gray wolf are similar in size to the teeth of the red wolf. Federico can run the 40-yard dash as quickly as Johanna can.

item.title: 8.2 Identifying Comparisons
item.description: Lesson Content There are a number of signal words that can alert us that a comparison is being made. in the same way than at the same rate similar to correspondingly much as so whereas while nevertheless nonetheless by the same token similarly just as likewise in similar fashion as … as 💡MUST KNOW: Familiarize yourself with the common comparison signal words and be on the lookout for them. Let’s now discuss the important concept that comparisons must be logical.

item.title: 8.3 Comparisons Must be Logical
item.description: Lesson Content Whenever we make a comparison, that comparison must be logical. Let’s discuss how these illogical comparisons typically occur. When an Item Is Illogically Compared With a Different Type of Item Some illogical comparisons involve an item compared with a different type of item. Consider the following sentence: Incorrect: The cost of summer camp is much higher than summer school. Let’s identify the two items being compared. We can’t logically compare summer school with the cost of summer camp.

item.title: 8.4 When Something is Mistakenly Compared With Itself
item.description: Lesson Content Another type of illogical comparison involves the comparison of something with itself. Incorrect: Julie is more skilled at singing than any singer I’ve encountered. This sentence expresses a comparison of Julie with any singer that the speaker has ever encountered. Since Julie cannot be more skilled than herself, we need to distinguish between Julie and “any singer.” We can do this by inserting the word “other,” as demonstrated below:

item.title: 8.5 Verbs in Comparisons
item.description: Lesson Content So far, whether examples that we have considered have been logical has depended primarily on whether comparing the things compared, which are named by nouns, makes sense. One thing that we can be sure of is that repeating in the second part of a comparison the verb or verb phrase that appears in the first part of a comparison is generally going to work. Jim attends the club meetings less regularly than Andrew attends the club meetings.

item.title: 8.6 Ellipsis in Comparisons
item.description: Lesson Content While the word “ellipsis” may not be familiar to you, ellipsis is not a complex concept. Consider the following example: The head of sales attends webinars more often than other team members. When we read the above sentence, we understand it to mean the following: The head of sales attends webinars more often than other team members attend webinars. In the first version, the wording “attend webinars” is omitted after “other team members.

item.title: 8.7 Ambiguous Comparisons
item.description: Lesson Content As we have seen, omitting repeated words in a sentence that expresses a comparison is OK as long as the sentence still expresses its meaning effectively. Consider the following sentence: Incorrect: Alex is much nicer to me than Alfredo. This sentence could be interpreted in two different ways. In order for the sentence to be effective, the sentence has to include words that help clarify the meaning. To convey the first meaning unambiguously, we can say:

item.title: 8.8 Incomplete Comparisons
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes a sentence leaves a comparison incomplete. Let’s look at an example: Incorrect: This tent is just as waterproof. The meaning of this sentence is ambiguous because of an incomplete comparison. This tent is just as waterproof as more expensive models. The comparison becomes more complete via the addition of a noun to which the existing noun is compared. Incorrect: This project will cost less. Cost less than what?

item.title: 8.9 Sentences That Express Comparisons That Are Incomplete Even Though the Items Meant to Be Compared Are All Mentioned
item.description: Lesson Content There is another move involving incomplete comparisons that the GMAT can make. Incorrect: When it is compared with eating butter, eating olive oil is healthier. Notice what’s going on in the above example. Yes, the sentence says “compared with eating butter,” but “compared with eating butter” is part of a modifying clause, “when it is compared with eating butter,” that modifies “eating olive oil is healthier.” So, read literally, the sentence conveys that eating olive oil is healthier than something else – the sentence does not say what else – at certain times.

item.title: 8.10 “Like” versus “Such As” and “Including”
item.description: Lesson Content The word “like” can be used in a comparison, but not to introduce an example. To see the difference between the use of “like” and the use of “such as” demonstrated, consider the example sentences below: Incorrect: When traveling through the Everglades, one may see many animals, like snakes, mammals, and alligators. Since “like” is strictly a comparison word on the GMAT, using it in this context would imply that one may see, not the animals mentioned, but rather animals similar to, or “like,” snakes, mammals, or alligators.

item.title: 8.11 “Like” versus “As”
item.description: Lesson Content Just as people often misuse the words “like” and “such as,” they also often use the word “like” when the word “as” is needed. Incorrect: The teacher acts like she were a drill sergeant. While “as” is a versatile word that can be used as a preposition or a conjunction, the comparison word “like” can be used only as a preposition. So, the above example does not make sense, since, in the above example, the object of “like” is a clause, “she were a drill sergeant.

item.title: 8.12 Comparisons That Involve Quantity Words and Countable or Uncountable Nouns
item.description: Lesson Content Some nouns are countable, meaning that they have plural forms and that their plural forms name quantities made up of countable items. Other nouns are uncountable, meaning that quantities named by these nouns are not made up of countable items. One reason why understanding the distinction between countable and uncountable nouns is useful in GMAT Sentence Correction is that the words used in expressing comparisons involving quantities, called “quantity words,” vary along with the types of quantities compared.

item.title: 8.13 “Number” versus “Amount”
item.description: Lesson Content We use “number” with countable nouns and “amount” with uncountable nouns. For example: The number of species worldwide is declining. Notice that “species” is a countable noun. The number of people at the beach was amazing. Of course, “people” is a countable noun. The amount of enthusiasm on display at the candidate’s rally was shocking. “Enthusiasm” can’t be counted. The amount of water in the beaker is not sufficient for this experiment.

item.title: 8.14 Correctly Using Quantity Words When Discussing Quantities of Time, Money, or Weight
item.description: Lesson Content Time, money, and weight may seem to be countable. For instance, we know that we would use “less” with “water,” as does the following example: The tank currently contains less than two gallons of water. Money works similarly, as we can see in the following example: The account currently holds less than two dollars. Time works similarly, as we can see in the following example: The sun will set less than five hours from now.

item.title: 8.15 “Greater Than” versus “More Than”
item.description: Lesson Content “Greater than” is used in comparing the sizes, levels, degrees, magnitudes, or extents of things. “More … than” is not synonymous with “larger than” or “higher than.” “More” can be used in multiple ways, including as a determiner that directly modifies what follows it. Consider the following example: Her dislike of gambling is greater than her dislike of exercise. Notice that, in this case, the magnitudes of her dislikes are compared.

item.title: 8.16 Maintaining Awareness of Whether Comparison Expressions Use Correct Combinations of Words
item.description: Lesson Content As we have seen, to a large degree, GMAT Sentence Correction is a test of skill in noticing issues. Another way in which Sentence Correction questions test the same skills is through the use of incorrect combinations of words in comparison expressions. So, in order to ensure that we will correctly answer Sentence Correction questions involving comparisons, we have to maintain awareness of whether the comparison expressions in the sentences use correct combinations of words.

item.title: 8.17 “Whereas,” “While,” and “Although” Are Different From “Unlike.”
item.description: Lesson Content It’s common for people to perceive “whereas” and “unlike” as somewhat synonymous or as functioning in similar ways in sentences. Now, we’ve already learned that, when we see a comparison that employs “unlike,” the rules governing such a comparison are fairly strict and that the two elements related by “unlike” have to be rather similar. Unlike Stacy Ann, Jovan enjoys photography. Notice that two people named by proper nouns, “Stacy Ann” and “Jovan” are compared.

item.title: 8.18 What Appears to Be the Best Written, Clearest Comparison May Be in an Incorrect Choice.
item.description: Lesson Content As we have discussed previously, in order to force test-takers to use logic in choosing among Sentence Correction choices rather than simply choose a choice that “sounds correct,” Sentence Correction question writers often seek to make incorrect choices seem correct and correct choices seem incorrect. Consider the following two sentence versions: Version 1: Having been hunted to near extinction in the wild, there are now more tigers living in captivity in zoos, sanctuaries, and private hands than there are living in the wild.
